coredata.dc:title: Detection of penicillin G residues in milk based on dual-emission carbon dots and molecularly imprinted polymers
coredata.dc:creator: Jalili, Roghayeh
Khataee, Alireza
Rashidi, Mohammad-Reza
Razmjou, Amir
coredata.dc:description: Instant detection of antibiotic residues in dairy products has been remained a challenge. Current methods require careful samples storage and handling, skilled personnel, and expensive instrumentations. Herein, we report the preparation of a ratiometric fluorescent sensor that contains different colored Carbon dots (CDs) as dual fluorophores, and a mesoporous structured molecularly imprinted polymer as a receptor (B/YCDs@mMIP) for penicillin-G (PNG) detection in milk. Upon PNG addition, only the fluorescence of yellow emissive CDs was quenched due to analyte blockage, while that of the blue emissive CDs stayed almost constant, which led to an obvious change in the fluorescence color from the yellow to blue. A linear response in the range of 1–32 nM with a detection limit of 0.34 nM and excellent recognition specificity for PNG over its analogs were also observed. Comparing our sensor with its counterparts, it exhibited a promising potential in the in-situ PNG detection in milk.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometry
Yellow emissive carbon dots
Penicillin G
Fluorescence sensor
Molecularly imprinted polymer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of the B/YCDs@mMIPs ratiometric sensor and control material
Construction and characterization of dual emission B/YCDs@mMIP ratiometric sensor
Stability and reproducibility of the sensor
Detection of PNG in milk samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Iran University of Medical Sciences for all the support provided (Project No: 97-4-75-13659 ). We also sincerely thank all support of University of Tabriz . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of (a) Y-CDs, (b) B-CDs and (c) the ratiometric sensor excited in 365 nm. The inset shows the photographs of B-CDs (right), Y-CDs (left) under 365 nm UV light.
(a) TEM and (b) SEM images of B/YCDs@mMIPs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the B/YCDs@mMIPs ratiometric sensor upon addition of different concentrations of PNG, (b) Corresponding linear plotting relationship between log ((F560/F440)0/(F560/F440)) and PNG concentration, and (c) Color changes of the B/YCDs@mMIPs ratiometric sensor in the presence of different concentrations of PNG. The experimental conditions were B/YCDs@mMIPs concentration, 30 mg/L; pH = 8 and response time, 5 min.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the B/YCDs@mNIPs ratiometric sensor upon addition of different concentrations of PNG, (b) Corresponding linear plotting relationship between log ((F560/F440)0/(F560/F440)) and PNG concentration, and (c) Color changes of the B/YCDs@mNIPs ratiometric sensor in the presence different concentrations of PNG. The experimental conditions were B/YCDs@mNIPs concentration, 30 mg/L; pH = 8 and response time, 5 min.
Schematic illustration of the fabrication process B/YCDs@mMIPs ratiometric sensor and the sensing mechanism of PNG.
Precision of the method (intra-day and inter-day).
Results for the determination of PNG in raw and pasteurized milk samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean of three determinations ± relative standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Detection of penicillin G residues in milk based on dual-emission carbon dots and molecularly imprinted polymers

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Health Promotion Research Center, Iran University of Medical Sciences, 1449614535 Tehran, Iran (A. Khataee). Department of Biotechnology, Faculty of Biological Science and Technology, University of Isfahan, Isfahan 73441-81746, Iran (A. Razmjou).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Instant detection of antibiotic residues in dairy products has been remained a challenge. Current methods require careful samples storage and handling, skilled personnel, and expensive instrumentations. Herein, we report the preparation of a ratiometric fluorescent sensor that contains different colored Carbon dots (CDs) as dual fluorophores, and a mesoporous structured molecularly imprinted polymer as a receptor (B/YCDs@mMIP) for penicillin-G (PNG) detection in milk. Upon PNG addition, only the fluorescence of yellow emissive CDs was quenched due to analyte blockage, while that of the blue emissive CDs stayed almost constant, which led to an obvious change in the fluorescence color from the yellow to blue. A linear response in the range of 1–32 nM with a detection limit of 0.34 nM and excellent recognition specificity for PNG over its analogs were also observed. Comparing our sensor with its counterparts, it exhibited a promising potential in the in-situ PNG detection in milk.

ce:section.ce:para: β-lactams are the most common antibiotics that extensively used in animal husbandry for the treatment of bacterial infections, often without the proper care as required for antibiotic usage. The extensive antibiotic use, without the proper care, may result in residues appearing in milk and dairy products. The presence of drug residues in dairy products is seriously threatening the health of consumers, which may cause generalized bacterial resistance and allergic side effects. In order to avoid these hazards, European Union (EU) Regulation has set maximum residue limits (MRLs) in the milk and meat for some antibiotics (Regulation, 2009). Milk has the lowest MRL value (12 nM, 4 µg/Kg) of residual penicillin G (benzylpenicillin) among other β-lactams. To date, two major methodologies including chromatographic assays (HPLC, LC-MS, etc.) (Van Royen, Dubruel, Van Weyenberg, & Daeseleire, 2016; Martínez-Huelamo, Jiménez-Gámez, Hermo, Barrón, & Barbosa, 2009) and immunoassay methods (Kwon, Yoo, Lee, & Jeon, 2018; Pennacchio et al., 2016) are employed for the detection of PNG. However, these methodologies require careful storage and handling of biomolecules, highly skilled personnel, lengthy analysis times and expensive and sophisticated instrumentation. Therefore, the development of a fast, simple, and specific assay method that could be easily applied to point-of-care detection is of great importance. Up to now, several fast and sensitive methods for the determination of PNG have been reported (Kwon et al., 2018; Lee et al., 2017; Moreira, Callera, Sotomayor, & Bueno, 2014).

Ratiometric fluorescence sensors, which detect the analytes based on measuring the ratio of fluorescence (FL) intensities under two well-resolved wavelengths, widely attracted attention (Song et al., 2017). Ratiometric probes provide an intrinsic built-in correction for avoiding environmental effects and thus provide advantages in terms of higher sensitivity for trace level analytes and accuracy. In addition, the signal variation of the ratiometric fluorescent probe can be easily observed by naked eyes which provides an advantage in point of care testing (Amjadi & Jalili, 2018; Su et al., 2018).

Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs), a new type of zero-dimensional nanomaterials in carbon family, have attracted tremendous attention as substitutes for long-known semiconductor quantum dots (QDs). CDs possess numerous interesting merits such as extremely low toxicity, high photo and chemical stability, good aqueous solubility and facile synthetic routes (Das, Bandyopadhyay, & Pramanik, 2018; Wang, Lu, Tang, & Xu, 2017). However, most of the reported CDs emitted blue-green luminescence and the lack of efficient long-wavelength emissive CDs highly restricted their potential applications in ratiometric fluorescent probes. Almost all of the reported quantum dot-based ratiometric fluorescent probes used QDs as luminescent materials (Wu, Guo, Liu, & Sun, 2015; Xu & Lu, 2015a, 2015b).

Molecularly imprinted technology, often described as a method for creating antibody-like artificial materials with tailor-made recognition sites, has become a well-established analytical tool for molecular recognition. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with many figures of merit such as excellent selectivity, high chemical and physical stability, simple and inexpensive preparation methods, are considered as promising substitutes for biological molecules as recognition elements in sensors. MIPs can be designed for virtually any target of interest. MIP based sensors have been successfully developed for the determination of pharmaceuticals (Amjadi & Jalili, 2016; Eskandari, Amirzehni, Asadollahzadeh, Hassanzadeh, & Eslami, 2018), pesticides (Amjadi & Jalili, 2017), viruses (Altintas et al., 2015; Malik, Nantasenamat, & Piacham, 2017), cells (Pan, Chen, Ma, & Pan, 2018), explosives (Hassanzadeh, Khataee, Mosaei Oskoei, Fattahi, & Bagheri, 2017), toxins (Zhao, Jia, Yu, & Sun, 2011) and etc.

In the present research, we reported for the first time the encapsulation of both yellow-emissive CDs (Y-CDs) and blue emissive CDs (B-CDs) into the mesoporous molecularly imprinted polymer to fabricate a visual sensor in a totally ‘greenway’. Considering the importance of sensitive and on-site detection of PNG, we used PNG as the target analyte. In detail, firstly B-CDs are incorporated into silica-spheres, and then Y-CDs embedded mesoporous imprinted polymer layer was obtained on the surface B-CDs@SiO2 by sol-gel method. The obtained nanocomposite displayed two distinct emission peaks at 560 nm and 440 nm under a single excitation wavelength which is used as response signal and reference signal, respectively. Upon the addition of PNG, yellow fluorescence gradually faded away, whereas the blue fluorescence slightly fluctuated. This fluorescence intensity ratio variation produces a continuous fluorescence color change from yellow-to-blue which can be discerned by the naked eye. The resulting ratios of fluorescence intensities at two wavelengths can be utilized for the trace level determination of PNG. It proves that the proposed ratiometric sensor is reliable and sensitive to directly detect PNG residue in milk.

O-phenylenediamines (o-PD), citric acid, urea, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) were obtained from Merck (Germany). Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (US). Penicillin G, Nafcillin (NFC), Dicloxacillin (DXN), Oxacillin (OXN) were kindly provided by Dana Pharmaceutical Co (Tabriz, Iran).

Fluorescence intensities and spectra were obtained by a Perkin Elmer LS45 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Waltham, USA) with a quartz cell (1 cm) with excitation and emission slit widths of 10 and 5 nm, respectively, and the excitation wavelength at 365 nm. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by Shimadzu UV2550 spectrophotometer (Japan). The morphology of nanoparticles was determined by a scanning electron microscope (Tescan mira 3, Czech Republic) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with Perkin-Elmer Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. Surface areas were calculated via Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis by nitrogen adsorption/desorption by a Quantachrom C (USA) instrument.

The Y-CDs were prepared according to a published method (Wu et al., 2018). In detail, 500 mg of o-PD were dissolved in 50 mL of water. Then the solution is sealed into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After natural cooling, the obtained suspension was centrifuged (12000 r min−1, 15 min), to collect the precipitate. The precipitate was washed with ethanol and water for several times and then dissolved in 20 mL water to obtain or Y-CDs aqueous solution, emitting yellow luminescence. The final concentration of YCDs suspension was 15 mg/mL.

Blue-emissive carbon dots were synthesized through the procedure as reported previously in the literature (Hai, Lin, Chen, & Wang, 2018). Briefly, 0.1 g ureaand 0.21 g citric acid were dissolved in 25 mL of water. The solution then was transferred into a Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature. The pH of the final product was adjusted at 7.0 with NaOH. The resultant B-CDs were diluted to 100 mL with water for further use. The final concentration of B-CDs suspension was 3 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the B/YCDs@mMIPs ratiometric sensor and control material
ce:section.ce:para: B/YCDs@mMIPs ratiometric sensor was synthesized via a one-pot sol-gel based method using TOES as a silica precursor and cross-linker. First, 300 µL of B-CDs and 200 μL of ammonia (25%) were injected into a 50 mL of 2:1 water-ethanol solution and stirred for 10 min. Then, 50 µL of TEOS was diluted to 10 mL with ethanol and add drop by drop to the above solution and continue stirring for 5 h. Then 500 µL of Y-CDs, 30 μL of APTES and 20 mg of PNG (template for molecular recognition) were added. After stirring for 30 min, 3 mL of CTAB (0.4 M, template for mesoporous structure), 0.1 mL of NaOH (0.5 M) and 0.1 mL of TEOS)in 10 mL ethanol(were added and the resulting mixture was stirred for 12 h. Subsequently, the obtained materials were precipitated by centrifugation. To remove PNG and CTAB, the precipitate was washed several times with water and ethanol mixture (1:1) with the aid of centrifugation-sonication cycles. The obtained B/YCDs@mMIPs were suspended in water and stored at 4 °C. For studying molecular imprinting process efficiency, mesoporous structured nonimprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor (B/YCDs@mNIPs) was synthesized in the exact same way, except that the template (PNG) was omitted. Also, the control PNG imprinted non-mesoporous structured ratiometric fluorescence (B/YCDs@MIPs) was prepared in the same procedure but without CTAB.

To a 5.0 mL volumetric flask, 200 μL of B/YCDs@mMIPs ratiometric sensor (with concentration of 50 mg/L), 100 μL of phosphate buffer solution (pH = 8, 0.1 M), and an appropriate amount of PNG standard or sample solution were added, then, the solutions were diluted to 5 mL with deionized water. The mixtures were kept at room temperature for at least 5 min before fluorescence measurements. The excitation wavelength was set at 365 nm and the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 560 and 440 nm (F 560/F 440) were measured. Three replications were performed for each tested value.

Raw and Pasteurized milk samples were purchased from local markets. The milk samples were spiked with PNG to final concentrations of 10, 20 and 30 nM. The mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min to eliminate protein. The fat layer was discarded and the supernatant was diluted 5 times with PBS (pH = 8) for PNG detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and characterization of dual emission B/YCDs@mMIP ratiometric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The construction of the dual emission B/YCDs@mMIP ratiometric sensor for the trace detection of PNG was illustrated in Scheme 1 . First, B-CDs embedded silica nanoparticles (B-CDs@SiO2) were synthesized via Stober method. Silica shell prevents the direct contact of the B-CDs with the external solvents, thus providing a reliable reference signal. Then imprinted mesoporous silica shell was formed on the surface of B-CDs@SiO2 via the co-condensation reaction of APTES (functional monomers) and TEOS (cross-linker) in the presence of the structure-directing surfactant (CTAB), analyte template (PNG) and Y-CDs. After removal of the template and CTAB, binding sites which are complementary in size, shape, and functionality to the analyte (template) remained in the cross-linked mesoporous polymer matrix, which is capable of rebinding the analyte with high specificity. The resulting mesoporous B/YCDs@mMIP had uniform mesoscale channels because CTAB surfactant templates the nanometer-sized pore formation.

The fluorescence spectra of Y-CDs, B-CDs and the ratiometric sensor are shown in Fig. 1 . As shown in Fig. 1a, the as-prepared Y-CDs exhibit the fluorescence maximum at 560 nm with the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, therefore they show strong yellow luminescence under UV light (inset of Fig. 1a). The quantum yield (QY) of Y-CDs is measured to be 15.2% by using Rhodamine B as fluorescence standard. When excited at 365 nm, the B-CDs exhibit a strong luminescence at 440 nm. For B-CDs, the QY was obtained using quinine sulfate as a standard (54% in 0.1 M H2SO4), which was found to be 45%. B-CDs display strong blue luminescence under UV light (inset of Fig. 1a). B/YCDs@mMIP ratiometric sensor showed two well-resolved emission bands at 440 nm and 560 nm under a single excitation wavelength of 365 nm which indicates that Y-CDs and B-CDs are successfully incorporated in the structure of ratiometric sensor.

The morphological structure of B/YCDs@mMIP was investigated by SEM and TEM. As shown in the TEM image (Fig. 2 a), B/YCDs@mMIP nanoparticles have a highly uniform spherical morphology with an average spherical size of 80 nm. Moreover, The SEM (Fig. 2b) results exhibited that the surface of B/YCDs@mMIP was obviously rough and has mesoporous nature.

Fig. S1 (Electronic Supplementary Materials) shows the FT-IR spectrum of the fabricated B/YCDs@mMIP ratiometric sensor. The characteristic peak around 461 and 784 cm−1 assigned to the Si–O vibrations and the wide and strong absorption band around 1055 cm−1, showed typical asymmetric Si–O–Si stretching, indicating the presence of SiO2 matrices. The stretching vibration bands of NH around 3400 and 1560 cm−1 and aliphatic CH stretching band at 2927 cm−1 suggests the presence of the aminopropyl group of APTES. These results illustrated that the MIP was generated from the sol–gel condensation of APTES and TEOS. Furthermore, the similarity of locations and appearance of the major bands of the B/YCDs@mNIP (Fig. S1, curves 1 and 2) and B/YCDs@mMIP suggests that the chemical compositions of the MIP and NIP were similar.

The interconnected mesoporous structure of B/YCDs@mMIPs was confirmed through BET experiment. The specific surface area of B/YCDs@mMIPs was obtained to be 1089.12 m2/g which is significantly high compared to the specific surface area of corresponding non-mesoporous structured B/YCDs@MIPs (27.13 m2/g). The Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) pore size distribution function calculated from the adsorption branch of the isotherms showed uniform mesoporous with an average diameter of 2.17 nm (Fig. S2). These BET and BJH values agree well with formerly reported surface area values for silica-based mesoporous MIPs (Xu & Lu, 2015a, 2015b).

To obtain the high sensitivity and fast analysis, some assay conditions including pH value, response time and amount of B/YCDs@mMIP which may affect the recognition performance of B/YCDs@mMIP, were investigated using quenching ratio described as F560/F440 (or FY-CDs/FB-CDs), as the index.

The pH value has a remarkable influence on the fluorescence intensity of B/YCDs@mMIPs and template rebinding efficiency. In the absence of PNG, the FL intensities of both peaks of B/YCDs@mMIPs were significantly stable in the pH interval between 4 and 10 (Fig. S3). The effect of pH values on the recognition of PNG (10 nM) by the B/YCDs@mMIP ratiometric sensor (fluorescence intensity ratio) was examined. The results showed that at pH 8.0, the quenching efficiency was the maximum. Thus the proper pH of rebinding was 8 (Fig. S4). PBS buffer (pH = 8, 10 mM) was used for the following work.

To evaluate the efficient interaction between PNG and the recognition site of B/YCDs@mMIPs, the response time of the fluorescence probe was investigated. The fluorescence intensity ratio was observed at different reaction time after adding 10 nM of PNG. As shown in Fig. S5 fluorescence intensity of mesoporous B/YCDs@mMIPs decreased rapidly in the initial 3 min and achieved a plateau within 5 min. So the response time of the mesoporous ratiometric sensor can be considered 5 min. In comparisonwith reported non-mesoporous surface imprinted QDs sensors which usually had the response time about 40 min, this sensor has an incredibly rapid response (Chullasat, Nurerk, Kanatharana, Davis, & Bunkoed, 2018; Liu et al., 2013; Wei et al., 2015). Mesoporous structure of imprinted layer reduces the mass transfer resistance and accelerates the rebinding of target molecules thus decreases the response time.

The concentration of B/YCDs@mMIPs had a significant effect on the quenching efficiency. It was found that when the concentration of B/YCDs@mMIPs was changed from 10 to 60 mg/L, the highest quenching ratio was obtained at 30 mg/L (Fig. S6). So, this amount was used throughout the work.

Under the optimum conditions, the fluorescent and visual ability of the B/YCDs@mMIPs for the detection of PNG was further studied. As shown in Fig. 3 a, upon the addition of PNG, the fluorescence intensity of Y-QCDs (560 nm) was continuously quenched while the intensity of B-CDs (440 nm) remained relatively constant, leading to a ratiometric detection of PNG. As can be seen in Fig. 3b, there is a good linear relationship between the logarithm of fluorescence intensity ratio [i.e. log ((F560/F440)0/(F560/F440)), where (F560/F440)0 and (F560/F440) are the fluorescence intensity ratios before and after addition of PNG, respectively] and the PNG concentration in the range of 1–32 nM. The established linear equation (y = 0.0273C + 0.0037, R2 = 0.9977) used for the determination of PNG with the detection limit as low as 0.34 nM (3S/N). The reproducibility of the method was investigated by calculating intra-day and inter-day precision. Five replicate measurements were carried out at PNG concentrations of 5, 10 and 20 nM in the same day and under the same experimental conditions. Inter-day precision was evaluated by repeated analyses on 5 days using the same samples and then calculating the relative standard deviation (RSD). The results are shown in Table 1 . The obtained acceptable values were lower than the limits required for biological samples and confirmed high precision of the proposed ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor.

Thanks to the B-CDs as a reference, a decrease of Y-QCDs fluorescence intensity, leads an obvious change in the fluorescence color of B/YCDs@mMIPs from yellow to blue with increasing the PNG concentration (Fig. 3c).

To confirm the success of molecular imprinting process, as a control experiment, the dose-response of the B/YCDs@mNIPs to PNG has been carried out. As can be seen in Fig. 4 by the addition of PNG, the ratio of the fluorescent intensity of B/YCDs@mNIPs decreases, but higher LOD (3.3 nM) and narrower linear range (8–29 nM) were obtained. As a result, the quenching degree of yellow fluorescence intensity was not large enough to change colors and the visual color change of the test solution was inconspicuous (Fig. 4c). This phenomenon can be explained as no specific recognition site was formed in B/YCDs@mNIPs. Only those Y-CDs located near the surface of B/YCDs@mNIPs can be quenched. For this sensor, the imprinting factor (IF) which calculated as the ratio of the slopes of the linear relationship of ratiometric B/YCDs@mMIPs and B/YCDs@mNIPs was obtained of 5.2. This high IF manifested that the imprinting process can greatly enhance the quenching efficiency and sensitivity.

To demonstrate the superiority of the mesoporous structure of ratiometric fluorescence B/YCDs@mMIPs, non-mesoporous structured B/YCDs@MIPs ratiometric probe was also prepared as a comparison. The dose-response of the non-mesoporous structured ratiometric B/YCDs@MIPs to PNG was tested. The linear range, LOD and response time of this probe for PNG were 50–500 nM, 18 nM, and 32 min respectively, which indicates that the mesoporous structures play an important role in sensitivity enhancement and response rapidity. In mesoporous structured B/YCDs@MIPs, high pore volume and ultrathin wall thickness guarantee the generation of specific recognition sites near to the surface and hence provide high accessibility for the template molecule.

After adding PNG, the amino groups in APTES (functional monomer) could interact with PNG to form a complex through hydrogen bonding. Therefore, the nonradiative deactivation of excited-state Y-CDs via electron transfer process can occur as a result of the strong interaction between PNG and B/YCDs@MIPs. As displayed by the absorption spectra (Fig. S7), the UV absorption band of PNG is close to the bandgap of the Y-CDs. Therefore, as illustrated in (Fig. S8), the electrons at the conductive band of the Y-CDs can directly transfer to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of UV band of PNG, leading to the quenching of Y-CDs fluorescence.

To evaluate the selectivity of B/YCDs@MIPs ratiometric sensor responding to PNG, some structural PNG analogs (Fig. S9) including Nafcillin (NFC), Dicloxacillin (DXN) and Oxacillin (OXN) were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S10, the B/YCDs@MIPs response for PNG was significantly higher than other β-lactams. Along with, noticed fluorescence color changes were observed under UV irradiation for PNG. Also, the quenching amount of B/YCDs@NIPs was similar for PNG and its analogs. These results indicated the B/YCDs@MIPs have good selectivity for PNG detection.

The competitive binding experiments were also carried out. The concentration of PNG was fixed and the concentration of NFC was increased respectively. As showed in Fig. S11, when the concentration of PNG was fixed, the fluorescence response was slightly affected by the increase of the CNFC/CPNG ratio. This result confirms our MIP sensor had high resistance to interference from analogs. Because a lot of specific recognition sites were generated during the imprinting process of B/YCDs@MIPs which are complementary to PNG in terms of shape, size, and functional groups. Thus PNG binding occurs strongly and restricts the binding of others.

The effect of some common ions and various biologically important species in milk samples was also measured by analyzing a solution of PNG (10 nM) in the presence of excess amounts of interfering substances. As shown in Fig. S12, the tolerance limit was considered as the concentration of the added interfering species which cause a relative error of less than 5%. The tolerable molar ratios were over 1000-fold for K+, Na+, Zn2+, NH4 +, Ag+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cl-, PO4 3-, SO4 2-, F-, NO3 −, sucrose, glucose, maltose, lactose, histidine, uric acid, ascorbic acid, glutathione, l-cysteine, glycine, tryptophan. Based on quenching ratio values for 10 nM of PNG (C) in the presence of 1000 nM of each interfering species (Fig S12), the biased estimate (ᶓ,) of the analyte concentration were computed from the equation of the calibration plot (see Section 3.4) and then the relative error of the estimates was obtained by Eq. (1):
(1)
ε - c
c
The calculated errors (Table S1) of the method are less than the maximum allowed error (0.05), then one may say that the method is highly selective (Doroko, Verbic, & Horvai, 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reproducibility of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence stability of B/YCDs@mMIPs was also tested by illuminating them with UV light using a UV lamp of 365 nm and testing the fluorescence intensity of B/YCDs@mMIP solution in every 5 min time intervals. As shown in Fig. S13, the photoluminescence intensity decreased by less than 3.0% after 30 min. Besides, when the B/YCDs@mMIPs is stored at 4 °C, it provides almost consistent detection results after three weeks. The removal-rebinding cycle tests were repeated for 5 times with the same B/YCDs@mMIPs by eluting with methanol/water (1:1, v/v) solution to elute rebound PNG, The RSD for five repeated analyses was 2.1%. These results demonstrate that due to highly cross-linked polymeric silica matrix, the B/YCDs@mMIP ratiometric sensor displayed high chemical and physical stability and excellent reusability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PNG in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the self-calibration function of fluorescence intensities and specific recognition sites that were generated in the MIPs, this developed sensor can detect PNG in the complex samples. PNG does not be detected in the raw and pasteurized milk samples using gas chromatography(GC). To validate the applicability of the sensor, the proposed sensor was adapted to determine PNG concentration in real milk samples. The spiking concentrations were set at 10, 20 and 30 nM (almost 1 and 2 and 3 times of the MRL value, respectively). Then the spiked serum samples were analyzed using our method and recovery studies were performed. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries ranged from 98% to 103% and the relative standard deviation (RSD) values were all below 0.9% which indicated that the B/YCDs@mMIPs ratiometric sensor accurately detected PNG in milk samples.

The performance of our developed ratiometric sensor for the detection of PNG in milk samples was compared with some reported approaches (Table S2). Without using susceptible and highly expensive biorecognition molecules (antibody, aptamer, enzyme and DNA), or need to sophisticated biosensing procedures, the LOD values obtained by the present method are better than those of biomolecule based optical and electrochemical methods (Kwon et al., 2018; Lee et al., 2017; Moreira et al., 2014; Pennacchio et al., 2016; Thiago, Foguel, Gonc, & Sotomayor, 2015). Excitingly, the LOD of this method is of the same orderofmagnitude as LOD recorded by UHPLC-MS/MS (Liu, Wang, Jiang, & Du, 2011). Although the lower detection limits were achieved with MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry, it requires elaborated and sophisticated instrumentation, time-consuming sample preparation procedures and highly skilled personnel (Kim et al., 2016). Our developed B/YCDs@mMIPs ratiometric sensor is sensitive enough for practical uses to determine PNG in milk. Moreover this ratiometric fluorescent probe possesses advantages in terms of sensitive visual detection and rapid response.

In summary, a PNG imprinted mesoporous structured ratiometric fluorescent sensor has been constructed by using CDs with different emission wavelengths. The ratiometric fluorescent sensor was applied for the highly sensitive and selective determination and visual detection of PNG in milk samples. The selectivity of the sensor was guaranteed by the molecular imprinting approach, and the sensitivity of the sensor was provided by ratiometric sensing approach and mesoporous structure of the imprinted shell. Thus, this sensor is sensitive enough to detect PNG in milk with a complex matrix at MRL level. Also, this sensor composed of carbon dots and silica and is really ecofriendly. Our present research introduces an on-site, rapid, portable, user-friendly and eco-friendly antibiotic test that meets regulatory requirements of public health agencies and the dairy industry.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Iran University of Medical Sciences for all the support provided (Project No: 97-4-75-13659). We also sincerely thank all support of University of Tabriz.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.126172.

ce:section.ce:para:
